nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral reefs and reef-associated lagoons, with juveniles sheltering in dense coral or algal thickets and adults occupying more open reef areas.

decorPreferences: Live rock structures with grazing surfaces
Avoid valuable stony corals — will be grazed and damaged

diet.notes: The Bicolor Parrotfish is an obligate grazer that uses its fused beak-like dental plates to scrape coralline algae, turf algae, and live coral polyps from reef surfaces, contributing to natural bioerosion in the wild. In captivity it will generally accept marine algae sheets (nori), spirulina-based pellets, and prepared herbivore foods, but constant access to coralline-encrusted live rock for grazing is considered essential to maintain body condition and keep the dental plates properly worn. Several small feedings spread throughout the day are generally recommended rather than one or two large meals.

goodWith: Other large peaceful reef fish in public-aquarium-scale tanks
avoidWith: Stony corals (LPS and SPS — will be grazed)
Conspecifics in anything smaller than an exhibit-scale system

summary: The Bicolor Parrotfish is a large Indo-Pacific reef species that reaches 50–90 cm and grazes live coral and coralline algae as part of its natural feeding behavior. Juveniles sometimes enter the trade but rapidly outgrow home systems, making this a species suited only to public aquariums.
careNotes:
A note on the name: Cetoscarus bicolor (Rüppell, 1829) is now formally recognized as a Red Sea endemic reaching around 50 cm, while the wider Indo-Pacific form historically sold under the same common name was reclassified in 2010 as Cetoscarus ocellatus (Valenciennes, 1840) and grows to 80–90 cm. Because the aquarium trade has not widely adopted this split, most specimens sold as 'Bicolor Parrotfish' are likely C. ocellatus; husbandry guidance applies equally to both species.

Juveniles are visually striking — white-bodied with a bright orange band and a black-spotted head — and are occasionally imported as a result. They grow rapidly, however, and the adult terminal-phase male is a 70–90 cm fish in shades of green and pink. The combination of large adult size and specialized diet makes this a realistic candidate only for public aquariums and zoological institutions with exhibit-scale systems of 2,000 litres or more.

The Bicolor Parrotfish is not reef-safe. Like most large parrotfish, it actively scrapes coralline algae from reef surfaces and bites live coral polyps as part of normal feeding behavior, so any stony corals in the system will be damaged. Live rock with heavy coralline encrustation is the closest practical substitute and should be provided in quantity.

Like other parrotfish, this species sleeps inside a self-secreted mucus cocoon — a transparent envelope believed to mask the fish's scent from nocturnal predators such as moray eels. A quiet cave or overhang where it can retreat after lights-out is strongly recommended.

This species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals mature first as females (the 'initial phase') and the dominant fish in a group eventually transitions to a terminal-phase male, a change accompanied by a dramatic shift in coloration.

This species is susceptible to HLLE (head-and-lateral-line erosion), a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and lateral line, most often linked to nutritional deficiencies or poor water quality. A varied diet rich in marine algae and consistently high water quality are the primary preventive measures. It is generally considered tolerant of copper-based medications, but quarantine using tank transfer or hyposalinity is widely regarded as a preferable approach.
breedingNotes: The Bicolor Parrotfish has not been bred in captivity at any meaningful scale. In the wild, reproduction is oviparous (egg-laying), with terminal-phase males defending small harems of females and engaging in distinct pairing behavior during spawning. The combination of very large adult size, pelagic spawning (eggs and larvae that drift in open water), and complex early larval development makes home or commercial breeding essentially not viable.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Used as primary because LiveAquaria does not list this species (it is not commercially appropriate). FishBase supplied scientific name, family, depth range, temperature range, native range (Red Sea sensu stricto), and IUCN status.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Cetoscarus bicolor
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum adult size (80 cm SL), Indo-Pacific distribution under the broader trade-sense usage, and confirmed protogynous sequential hermaphroditism with initial/terminal phase color change. Also documented the C. bicolor / C. ocellatus split.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Cetoscarus ocellatus
additional.notes: Confirms the 2010 taxonomic split: Indo-Pacific form (formerly lumped with C. bicolor) is now C. ocellatus, 30–80 cm, depth 1–40 m, partly corallivorous. Provides the size range used here for the trade-sense entry.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Parrotfish
additional.notes: Family-level reference for the mucus-cocoon sleeping behavior, protogynous hermaphroditism with initial/terminal phases, coral and coralline grazing, and the consensus that large parrotfish are unsuitable for home marine aquaria.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeds via pelagic spawning within a harem structure; captive breeding is not considered viable.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches roughly 50–90 cm as a terminal-phase adult and grazes live coral and coralline algae as its primary food source. Realistic adult housing is a 2000 L+ exhibit-scale system with no stony corals — beyond the scope of any reasonable home marine aquarium. Frequently imported as small juveniles, but rapidly outgrows hobby tanks; appropriate only for public aquariums and zoological institutions.
